| Channels / Configuration | channels/qa-channel | L23 Config | new_category | QA channel | Configuration and access policy | Account schema and policy gates | agent_decision | The `Config` section introduces a dedicated schema and operator knobs for QA Channel (`baseUrl`, `botUserId`, `botDisplayName`, `pollTimeoutMs`, `allowFrom`, `groupPolicy`, `groupAllowFrom`, per-room `requireMention`, `defaultTo`, `actions.*`, `accounts`, `defaultAccount`). Existing taxonomy covers similar concepts generically, but no current surface/category owns this channel-specific config surface. |

| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | (page) | new_feature | Channel framework | Outbound Delivery and Reply Pipeline | Configurable cross-surface retry policy | agent_decision | The page defines a shared retry policy contract, defaults, jitter, and per-provider/channel configuration knobs for outbound operations. | Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | L9 Goals | new_feature | Channel framework | Outbound Delivery and Reply Pipeline | Configurable cross-surface retry policy | agent_decision | Retry goals such as per-request retries, ordering preservation, and non-idempotent safety are part of the shared retry-policy capability and are not explicitly represented today. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | L15 Defaults | new_feature | Channel framework | Outbound Delivery and Reply Pipeline | Configurable cross-surface retry policy | agent_decision | Global retry defaults like attempts, max delay, jitter, and channel-specific minimum delay are operator-facing policy controls not called out in current shared delivery features. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | L24 Behavior | new_feature | Channel framework | Outbound Delivery and Reply Pipeline | Configurable cross-surface retry policy | agent_decision | The behavior section is the shared policy entrypoint for how retries are applied across model providers and channel transports. That shared policy layer is not currently scoreable as a distinct feature. |

| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | L45 Telegram | needs_docs_link_only | Telegram | Outbound Rendering Streaming and Reply Delivery | Durable outbound message recording | exact_override | Add docs link docs/concepts/retry.md to existing triplet coverage. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | L51 Configuration | new_feature | Channel framework | Outbound Delivery and Reply Pipeline | Configurable cross-surface retry policy | agent_decision | The configuration section documents explicit shared retry knobs in `openclaw.json` for channel transports, which the current taxonomy does not model as a standalone operator capability. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | L78 Notes | new_feature | Channel framework | Outbound Delivery and Reply Pipeline | Configurable cross-surface retry policy | agent_decision | Per-request retry scope and non-retry of completed composite steps are part of the shared retry-policy contract rather than already-modeled channel-specific features. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/retry | L83 Related | mapped_existing | Agent runtime and provider execution | Provider Recovery and Diagnostics | Rate-limit and capacity recovery | agent_decision | The Related section only points to existing failover and queue docs and does not add new capability coverage beyond current provider recovery and delivery behavior. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | (page) | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | The page defines the shared in-process queue for inbound auto-reply runs, lane behavior, and the operator-facing `/queue` contract. That queue/steering capability is not explicitly represented in current runtime taxonomy. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L11 Why | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | The rationale for serializing runs by session and capping shared concurrency describes the same missing queue capability, not an already-scored execution detail. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L16 How it works | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Lane-aware FIFO draining, session-key locking, global lanes, and enqueue timing behavior are central parts of the queue subsystem and are not broken out in the current feature list. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L24 Defaults | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Default queue mode, debounce window, queue cap, and drop policy are operator-visible queue controls that do not have explicit scorecard coverage today. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L38 Queue modes | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | The `steer`, `followup`, `collect`, and `interrupt` modes are concrete user-facing behaviors of active-run handling and should be modeled directly instead of being implied by generic execution coordination. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L68 Queue options | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Queue options such as debounce, cap, and drop policy are core operator controls for the queue subsystem and are not captured by existing runtime features. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L81 Steer and streaming | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | The interaction between steering and streaming, including runtime-boundary behavior and fallback from same-turn steering to later followups, is part of the missing shared queue/steering capability. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L95 Precedence | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Mode and option precedence across per-session overrides, per-channel config, and global defaults is a queue-specific operator contract not represented in the current taxonomy. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L110 Per-session overrides | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Persisted per-session `/queue` overrides are part of the shared queue control surface and are not modeled by current execution or session-routing features. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue | L116 Scope and guarantees | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Queue scope, per-session guarantees, lane separation for cron and nested work, and process-level concurrency bounds are central queue semantics that do not have explicit scorecard coverage. |

| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue-steering | L16 Runtime boundary | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Tool-boundary draining, turn-end emission, Codex `turn/steer` batching, and runtimes that reject same-turn steering are core steering semantics missing from the current feature list. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue-steering | L44 Modes | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | The mode matrix here is the same missing shared queue capability: how `steer`, `followup`, `collect`, and `interrupt` behave while a run is active. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue-steering | L53 Burst example | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | The burst example documents practical steering and batching semantics for multiple inbound prompts during tool execution, which are not explicitly scoreable today. |
| Agents / Messages and delivery | concepts/queue-steering | L66 Scope | new_feature | Agent runtime and provider execution | Agent Turn Execution | Active-run queue modes and steering | agent_decision | Scope limits such as steering staying within the active session and preserving the active run's policy are part of the same missing steering feature. |

| Capabilities / Building plugins | plugins/cli-backend-plugins | L29 What the plugin owns | mapped_existing | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | CLI backend plugins | CLI backend plugin overview | agent_decision | `What the plugin owns` introduces the three-part CLI backend plugin contract: package entry, manifest ownership, and runtime registration. That is the narrative overview for the missing CLI backend plugin category rather than Anthropic-specific runtime behavior. |
| Capabilities / Building plugins | plugins/cli-backend-plugins | L43 Minimal backend plugin | new_feature | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | CLI backend plugins | CLI backend registration and ownership | agent_decision | The minimal example covers `openclaw.plugin.json` `cliBackends`, `setup.cliBackends`, `activation`, and `api.registerCliBackend(...)` ownership rules. That authoring workflow is not represented by existing packaging or provider-tool features. |
| Capabilities / Building plugins | plugins/cli-backend-plugins | L171 Config shape | new_feature | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | CLI backend plugins | CLI backend config shape | agent_decision | This section defines the `CliBackendConfig` contract: command/argv, parser modes, prompt transport, session handling, image support, serialization, and watchdog tuning. Current taxonomy does not capture the author-facing config schema for CLI backend plugins. |
| Capabilities / Building plugins | plugins/cli-backend-plugins | L196 Advanced backend hooks | new_feature | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | CLI backend plugins | CLI backend execution hooks | agent_decision | `normalizeConfig`, `resolveExecutionArgs`, `prepareExecution`, `transformSystemPrompt`, `textTransforms`, auth epoch behavior, and native-tool mode are backend-specific execution hooks that do not fit existing generic authoring or provider-plugin features. |
| Capabilities / Building plugins | plugins/cli-backend-plugins | L215 MCP tool bridge | new_feature | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | CLI backend plugins | CLI backend MCP tool bridge | agent_decision | The page documents `bundleMcp`, `bundleMcpMode`, supported bridge modes, and fail-closed interaction with `nativeToolMode`. That is a discrete CLI backend authoring capability not modeled in the current Plugin SDK taxonomy. |
| Capabilities / Building plugins | plugins/cli-backend-plugins | L245 User configuration | new_feature | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | CLI backend plugins | CLI backend user override merge | agent_decision | This section documents how runtime defaults from the plugin are merged with user configuration under `agents.defaults.cliBackends.<id>` and how model fallbacks consume those ids. That user-override merge behavior is specific to CLI backend plugins and is not represented elsewhere. |
| Capabilities / Building plugins | plugins/cli-backend-plugins | L274 Verification | new_feature | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | CLI backend plugins | CLI backend verification and live smoke | agent_decision | The verification guidance is specific to CLI backend plugins: targeted builder/setup tests, `plugins inspect --runtime`, real model turns, and extra live smoke for image or MCP paths. Existing testing taxonomy is too generic to express this backend-specific proof surface. |

| Capabilities / Automation | automation/standing-orders | (page) | new_category | Automation: cron, hooks, tasks, polling | Standing Orders | Standing Orders | agent_decision | `docs/automation/index.md` already lists Standing Orders as a first-class automation mechanism, but the automation surface has no Standing Orders category yet. Add a category here instead of forcing this page into polling, cron, or heartbeat coverage. |
| Capabilities / Automation | automation/standing-orders | L14 Why standing orders | mapped_existing | Automation: cron, hooks, tasks, polling | Standing Orders | Standing Orders | agent_decision | This section motivates the same missing standing-orders mechanism: durable autonomous authority, less prompting, and exception-based escalation. |
| Capabilities / Automation | automation/standing-orders | L30 How they work | mapped_existing | Automation: cron, hooks, tasks, polling | Standing Orders | Standing Orders | agent_decision | It defines workspace-file storage, session injection, triggers, approval gates, and escalation rules, which is the core behavior the Standing Orders category should own. |
| Capabilities / Automation | automation/standing-orders | L47 Anatomy of a standing order | mapped_existing | Automation: cron, hooks, tasks, polling | Standing Orders | Standing Orders | agent_decision | The template with authority, trigger, approval gate, escalation, and execution steps is direct evidence for the Standing Orders mechanism rather than cron or task-ledger behavior. |
| Capabilities / Automation | automation/standing-orders | L72 Standing orders plus cron jobs | mapped_existing | Automation: cron, hooks, tasks, polling | Standing Orders | Standing Orders | agent_decision | Cron already exists, but this section is specifically about cron enforcing standing orders, so it belongs under the Standing Orders category instead of the cron lifecycle category alone. |
| Capabilities / Automation | automation/standing-orders | L98 Examples | mapped_existing | Automation: cron, hooks, tasks, polling | Standing Orders | Standing Orders | agent_decision | The examples section is illustrative evidence for the same standing-orders mechanism rather than a separate capability family. |

| Platforms / Platforms overview | platforms/easyrunner | (page) | needs_docs_link_only | Docker / Podman hosting | Provider-hosted VPS and Operator Runbooks | Provider/VPS Docker hosting docs | agent_decision | The page is an EasyRunner-specific hosted-container runbook for deploying OpenClaw behind a reverse proxy with persistent volumes. That operator path already belongs to the existing provider-hosted container guidance; taxonomy coverage is missing this docs page, not the capability. |
| Platforms / Platforms overview | platforms/easyrunner | L14 Before you begin | needs_docs_link_only | Docker / Podman hosting | Provider-hosted VPS and Operator Runbooks | Provider/VPS Docker hosting docs | agent_decision | The prerequisites section is standard hosted-container runbook material: domain routing, published image, persistent config/workspace volumes, and gateway auth prerequisites. It extends the existing provider-hosted operator guidance rather than introducing a new feature. |
| Platforms / Platforms overview | platforms/easyrunner | L26 Compose app | needs_docs_link_only | Docker / Podman hosting | Docker Install, Compose, and First-run Setup | Docker Compose gateway | agent_decision | This is another compose-based gateway deployment recipe, just expressed for EasyRunner's Podman-compatible app model and Caddy labels. The compose bring-up capability already exists in taxonomy. |
| Platforms / Platforms overview | platforms/easyrunner | L58 Configure OpenClaw | needs_docs_link_only | Security, auth, pairing, and secrets | Gateway Auth and Network Exposure | Trusted-proxy identity | agent_decision | The section's distinctive guidance is reverse-proxy-safe gateway auth: require shared auth, keep the gateway behind the proxy, and configure trusted-proxy handling correctly instead of disabling checks. That is existing gateway auth/network-exposure behavior applied to EasyRunner. |
| Platforms / Platforms overview | platforms/easyrunner | L79 Verify | needs_docs_link_only | Docker / Podman hosting | Networking, Control Ui, Health, and Observability | Container health endpoints | agent_decision | Verification is the normal hosted-container observability workflow: probe/status from a workstation and inspect host logs for startup/auth failures. The taxonomy already covers health, logs, and container runtime checks. |
| Platforms / Platforms overview | platforms/easyrunner | L91 Updates and backups | needs_docs_link_only | Docker / Podman hosting | Provider-hosted VPS and Operator Runbooks | Operator-facing update | agent_decision | Pull/build, redeploy, volume backup, workspace backup, and post-update doctor checks are all existing hosted-container operational lifecycle guidance. EasyRunner adds another platform example, not a new scorecard capability. |
| Platforms / Platforms overview | platforms/easyrunner | L99 Troubleshooting | needs_docs_link_only | Docker / Podman hosting | Provider-hosted VPS and Operator Runbooks | Operator-facing update | agent_decision | Connectivity, auth rotation, restored-volume ownership, and plugin/channel dependency failures are container-hosting operator repair topics already covered by the provider-hosted runbook category. The gap is that EasyRunner troubleshooting is not linked from current taxonomy docs. |

| Platforms / macOS companion app | platforms/mac/skills | (page) | new_feature | macOS companion app | Settings, Health, Channels, and Diagnostics | Gateway-backed skills management | agent_decision | The page documents a user-facing macOS app settings area for browsing skill eligibility, running gateway-host installs, and editing per-skill API key/env state. The current category covers settings navigation and channels/health, but it does not yet model Skills settings as a scoreable capability. |
| Platforms / macOS companion app | platforms/mac/skills | L11 Data source | mapped_existing | macOS companion app | Settings, Health, Channels, and Diagnostics | Gateway-backed skills management | agent_decision | The Data source section explains that the macOS app reads `skills.status` and `metadata.openclaw.requires` from the gateway to show eligibility and missing requirements. That is core status/requirements behavior for the missing Skills-management feature rather than a separate category. |
| Platforms / macOS companion app | platforms/mac/skills | L17 Install actions | mapped_existing | macOS companion app | Settings, Health, Channels, and Diagnostics | Gateway-backed skills management | agent_decision | Install actions describe the existing app workflow for invoking `skills.install`, honoring installer preferences, surfacing download-only choices, and fail-closing on dangerous findings. | Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | (page) | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | The page is a dedicated operator guide for routing runtime HTTP/WebSocket egress through an external filtering proxy, which is not represented by any current Gateway runtime feature. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L13 Why use a proxy | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | This section defines the operator-facing value proposition for managed proxy egress control, DNS rebinding defense, and centralized SSRF hardening, all of which belong to the missing outbound proxy capability. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L26 How OpenClaw routes traffic | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | The routing contract for Gateway, node, and local agent traffic, including loopback control-plane exceptions, is core behavior of the missing managed proxy feature. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L54 Related proxy terms | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | The terminology split between outbound forward-proxy routing, inbound trusted-proxy auth, and tool-specific env proxies documents the feature boundary that taxonomy does not currently capture. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L62 Configuration | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | The explicit `proxy.enabled`, `proxy.proxyUrl`, and TLS configuration surface is operator-visible product behavior and should be scored as part of the missing proxy feature. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L88 Gateway Loopback Mode | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | `proxy.loopbackMode` is a specific policy knob for managed proxy routing and does not fit existing inbound network-exposure or pairing features. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L118 Proxy Requirements | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | Proxy-side policy requirements are part of the operator contract for the managed egress-control feature and are not covered by current security-control feature names. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L133 Recommended blocked destinations | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | The denylist guidance is feature-specific operational policy for the managed outbound proxy path rather than general Gateway exposure guidance. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L160 Validation | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | `openclaw proxy validate` is the verification and diagnostics path for this specific proxy-routing capability, so it should live with the missing feature rather than generic health checks. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L214 Proxy CA trust | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | Private-CA trust for the proxy endpoint is scoped behavior of managed proxy routing, not the existing TLS-pinning or inbound-auth categories. |
| Gateway & Ops / Security | security/network-proxy | L249 Limits | new_feature | Gateway runtime | Security Controls | Managed outbound proxy routing | agent_decision | The limits section defines the supported boundary of managed process-level proxy routing and confirms this is a distinct Gateway security capability. |

| Gateway & Ops / Nodes and media | nodes/location-command | L9 TL;DR | mapped_existing | Gateway runtime | Nodes and Remote Capabilities | Remote device capabilities | agent_decision | The TL;DR is a summary of existing location-capability behavior: `location.get` runs through `node.invoke`, is off by default, and exposes a precise-location toggle. |
| Gateway & Ops / Nodes and media | nodes/location-command | L16 Why a selector (not just a switch) | needs_docs_link_only | Android app | Settings, Permissions, and Diagnostics | Settings sheet | agent_decision | The selector rationale is Android-facing permission UX for `off \| whileUsing` plus precise-location behavior against OS grant levels. That capability already exists, but the Android settings category currently cites only `docs/platforms/android.md`. |
| Gateway & Ops / Nodes and media | nodes/location-command | L26 Settings model | mapped_existing | Android app | Settings, Permissions, and Diagnostics | Settings sheet | agent_decision | The `location.enabledMode` and `location.preciseEnabled` model is existing Android settings and permission-handling behavior rather than a new feature family. |

| Help / Start here | help/troubleshooting | L11 First 60 seconds | needs_docs_link_only | CLI | CLI Observability | Status snapshots | agent_decision | The first-minute ladder is the condensed operator front door for existing `status`, gateway probe/status, doctor, channel probe, and log-tail workflows already modeled under CLI observability. | Help / Start here | help/troubleshooting | L37 Assistant feels limited or missing tools | needs_docs_link_only | Browser automation and exec/sandbox tools | Tool Policy and Sandbox Tool Gates | Tool Policy and Sandbox Tool Gates | agent_decision | This section explains `tools.profile`, per-agent tool overrides, and how to recheck effective tooling when file/shell/browser capabilities look missing. This page is missing as direct source evidence. |
| Help / Start here | help/troubleshooting | L83 Plugin install fails with missing openclaw extensions | needs_docs_link_only | Plugin SDK and bundled plugin architecture | Packaging plugins | Package metadata | agent_decision | The failure mode is specifically the required `package.json` `openclaw.extensions` contract for packaged plugins. That packaging rule already exists in taxonomy and only needs this troubleshooting section linked. |
| Help / Start here | help/troubleshooting | L108 Plugin present but blocked by suspicious ownership | needs_docs_link_only | ClawHub | Operator Inventory, Inspect, Doctor, and Troubleshooting | Troubleshooting stale config | agent_decision | Suspicious ownership, blocked plugin candidates, Docker bind-mount repair, and `doctor --fix` are existing operator troubleshooting and managed-plugin repair behavior, not a new taxonomy area. |
| Help / Start here | help/troubleshooting | L142 Decision tree | nav_missing_source |  |  |  | agent_decision | The decision tree fans out into no-reply, Control UI, gateway, channel, automation, node, and browser troubleshooting paths, so it should be treated as cross-surface routing rather than direct evidence for one scored capability. |

| Help / Start here | help/debugging | (page) | new_category | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling overview | agent_decision | The page documents a coherent operator/developer debugging surface including runtime debug toggles, per-session trace controls, plugin lifecycle timing, CLI and gateway profiling, raw-stream capture, and IDE/source-map debugging. Current observability taxonomy covers doctor, health, logs, metrics, and exports, but not these local debug workflows. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L12 Runtime debug overrides | new_feature | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | Runtime debug overrides | agent_decision | The `/debug` command provides runtime-only config mutation for troubleshooting without editing disk config. That operator-facing debug control is not represented in current observability categories. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L29 Session trace output | new_feature | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | Per-session trace toggles | agent_decision | The `/trace` workflow exposes plugin-owned trace lines for one session without enabling broad verbose logging. That scoped trace control is a real debugging capability gap in the existing taxonomy. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L46 Plugin lifecycle trace | new_feature | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | Plugin lifecycle phase tracing | agent_decision | The `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_LIFECYCLE_TRACE=1` path emits phase-by-phase timing for plugin install/refresh work. Current plugin and observability categories cover runtime behavior and diagnostics broadly, but not this dedicated lifecycle timing trace. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L72 CLI startup and command profiling | new_feature | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | CLI startup and sync-I/O profiling | agent_decision | Startup benchmarks, CPU profile capture, and sync-I/O tracing for CLI commands are explicit debug/profiling workflows absent from the current observability taxonomy. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L103 Gateway watch mode | needs_docs_link_only | CLI | Gateway Service Management | Foreground gateway runs | agent_decision | Watch mode is a developer-focused wrapper around the existing foreground gateway run lifecycle: start/restart, attach/detach, restart-on-change, and one-shot doctor repair. Existing CLI gateway management coverage is the right owner; this page just adds supporting evidence. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L189 Dev profile + dev gateway (--dev) | needs_docs_link_only | CLI | Gateway Service Management | Foreground gateway runs | agent_decision | The dev profile and `gateway --dev` section is still about running an isolated local gateway with disposable config/state and reset behavior. That belongs to the existing foreground/developer gateway workflow rather than a new taxonomy area. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L252 Raw stream logging (OpenClaw) | new_feature | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | Raw provider stream capture | agent_decision | Raw assistant-stream logging before formatting/filtering is a concrete debugging workflow for provider and runtime investigation that is not represented by current logging/export categories. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L281 Raw OpenAI-compatible chunk logging | mapped_existing | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | Raw provider stream capture | agent_decision | OpenAI-compatible chunk logging is the transport-specific variant of the same raw stream capture capability introduced in the previous section, not a separate taxonomy feature. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L300 Safety notes | mapped_existing | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | Raw provider stream capture | agent_decision | The safety notes are operational constraints for the same raw-log capture workflow: prompts, tool output, secrets, and PII can land in the logs, so sharing requires scrubbing rather than a separate capability. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L306 Debugging in VSCode | new_feature | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | VSCode source-map debugging | agent_decision | The page documents a supported source-map-based IDE debugging workflow with launch configurations for stepping through Gateway TypeScript. That contributor debugging path is not captured by existing observability taxonomy. |
| Help / Start here | help/debugging | L313 Setup | mapped_existing | Observability | Developer debugging, tracing, and profiling workflows | VSCode source-map debugging | agent_decision | The Setup subsection is the concrete enablement sequence for the same source-map IDE debugging capability introduced by the parent section. |

| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L19 What we protect | mapped_existing | Release engineering and CI validation | Local test, benchmark, and smoke workflows | Update and plugin validation guide | agent_decision | `What we protect` is the contract summary for the same maintainer validation guide: tarball completeness, upgrade-state preservation, doctor-owned cleanup, plugin install-source coverage, dependency cleanup, and unchanged plugin-update stability. |
| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L39 Local proof during development | new_surface | Release engineering and CI validation | Local test, benchmark, and smoke workflows | Local update and plugin regression gate | agent_decision | This section defines the local pre-Docker proof stack for update/plugin changes: `changed:lanes`, changed checks/tests, focused seam tests, and `release:check` tarball validation. That maintainer gate is not represented by existing product-surface taxonomy. |
| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L67 Docker lanes | needs_docs_link_only | Docker / Podman hosting | Docker e2e Release Smoke and Scheduler | Release-path install | agent_decision | The Docker-lane catalog documents existing package install/update proof in Linux containers, including plugin lifecycle, upgrade-survivor, published-upgrade-survivor, update-restart-auth, and update-migration lanes. That is existing Docker release-smoke coverage and mainly needs this page added as supporting docs. |
| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L151 Package Acceptance | new_surface | Release engineering and CI validation | Preflight, validation boxes, and publish orchestration | Package Acceptance release gate | agent_decision | The section defines candidate-source resolution, `package-under-test` artifact identity, suite profiles, Docker lane selection, published-baseline expansion, and manual workflow entrypoints for installable-package validation. No current maturity-scorecard surface owns that release-validation control plane. |
| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L234 Release default | new_surface | Release engineering and CI validation | Preflight, validation boxes, and publish orchestration | Release candidate proof stack | agent_decision | This section sets the default release-candidate evidence stack across changed gates, `release:check`, Package Acceptance, cross-OS checks, live suites, and Testbox usage. That cross-system approval workflow is a release-validation concern that existing runtime/install/plugin surfaces do not model. |
| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L250 Legacy compatibility | mapped_existing | Release engineering and CI validation | Preflight, validation boxes, and publish orchestration | Package Acceptance release gate | agent_decision | The time-boxed compatibility exceptions are policy detail for the same package-acceptance gate: specific published package versions may warn or skip known historic metadata gaps, while later releases must fail those defects. |
| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L265 Adding coverage | new_surface | Release engineering and CI validation | Local test, benchmark, and smoke workflows | Test-layer selection for update and plugin changes | agent_decision | The section gives contributor guidance for where to add coverage based on the failure seam: unit tests, package inventory checks, Docker CLI lanes, published-upgrade scenarios, update-owned restart proof, registry fixtures, and dependency-layout assertions. That validation-design workflow is not represented in the current taxonomy. |
| Help / Testing | help/testing-updates-plugins | L286 Failure triage | new_surface | Release engineering and CI validation | Local test, benchmark, and smoke workflows | Validation artifact triage and rerun guidance | agent_decision | This section documents how maintainers debug failed update/plugin validation by starting from package identity, then inspecting Docker artifacts, upgrade-survivor summaries, lane logs, and exact rerun commands. That artifact-driven triage workflow is a missing validation-surface capability. |
